speaker: interviewer
text: --Koedam, November 21st, 1996. And if you could tell us who is in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, Dophie, again. This is a very pretty picture. She was an adorable child. And we loved her very much. And she loved us, just as like her daddy and her mommy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- and you were hiding her for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hiding her with her parents for six months, until they have to find another place, since my brother was picked up by the Gestapo. And he was involved in the situation we were in. So we have to find another place. And it took a little while, but they found another place until the war is over. And we were very happy they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, these are our 10 grandchildren. I will go from the left to right. The first one is Monica. The second one is her friend. The third one is Robert. And then we have Chris. And then we have Michael. And we have Susan, Donald, Karen, Dawn, and Sharon. That was taken at our 50th anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this is a war monument placed in Rotterdam. For everybody, and ourself, it looks very ugly, and that is just what it was. That is so ugly. That represents the town of Rotterdam where the heart is taken out. You can see that it's open in the body. And the sculptor was Zadkine. That is Z-A-D-K-I-N-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you-- could you tell us what this-- what you've chosen the picture for? It's on the cover of-- on your book.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture I had that copy of-- because that says here And that was a manuscript, original, for my children. They had always ask me-- tell me about more of the war. And why don't you write about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is what I did. Here's the script. And on the bottom you see by Aplonia Bastiannse Koedam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Would you introduce us to the family you have with you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, sure. I want to introduce my husband, Cor. We always call him Cor; and our daughter, Monica, and our dog, Joshua.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] And, well, Monica, I'd like to ask you how-- how you feel about your parents' activities in the war and that the fact that they're telling their story.

speaker: interviewee
text: MONICA (DAUGHTER): Oh. Their activities in the war-- like, the best thing I can say is I'm extremely proud of them. It's something that I-- I tell other people about. I think it has brought of all of us up to have respect for all, you know, races and people. I mean, you know, our prejudices, are-- I don't think we have them because of what my parents have done-- that we are so defensive of anyone trying to choose a race and-- and-- and stigmatizing anyone because of our-- our background. You know, we just were bought up that every human being is a human being to itself. And respect them for that. And as far as-- I would say when I was younger, my parents really didn't talk about it much. You know, really, I guess like most-- many people didn't talk about the war too much in the early years. But her writing everything down-- and my father, he has assisted her tremendously-- a big support with everything he's done. I think it's wonderful that all this is written down. And it's just not my-- my parents and-- but their whole family-- have been just-- what they were willing to sacrifice, is, I think, just beyond belief that, you know, sharing every little morsel of food they possibly could have, and-- but there was no thought about it. And I hope I can be like that someday if I ever need to be. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Koedam, sir, anything that you would like to make a statement on your-- on behalf of your-- on your wife-- [LAUGHS] --about your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give it a go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I do really don't know what I would have to say about her-- only that we know each other so many years that we know each other without knowing each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I just-- I'm very proud of her what she had done here. And it took-- and a lot of work to get a book-- booklet-- of the-- of the manuscript together; and look, really as she had during the war, when our children were born, and she had a day book from all the babies. And we had a lot of information out of that book, especially the two boys. Because they had really experienced the war so very much as an-- as a baby can and as an two-- two-year-old boy. And I think that was a tremendous thing what she did. And we did it mainly because our children were-- as one who survived over our-- our experience during the war. And now they can have it upon their own-- in their own book. You can read it. And I'm glad that we can be-- that we could do it, that we were able to help those people to survive. I think that is what I mainly want to say. And I'm glad too that it will be recorded for their future.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Thank you.
